name: Jewish Wisdom
description: Teachings on justice, learning, and repair of the world from Torah, Talmud, and the sages.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: If I am not for myself, who will be for me? And being for my own self, what am I? And if not now, when?
quotes.notes: Hillel's threefold call to self-responsibility, concern for others, and urgent action, from the Ethics of the Fathers.

quotes.content: That which is hateful to you, do not do to your fellow. That is the whole Torah; the rest is commentary. Go and learn it.
quotes.notes: Hillel's reply to a would-be convert who asked to be taught the whole Torah while standing on one foot; the Golden Rule in its negative form.

quotes.content: Whoever saves a single life is considered by Scripture to have saved an entire world.
quotes.notes: From the Mishnah's teaching on the infinite worth of a single human being; rendered from the standard text of the Babylonian Talmud.

quotes.content: You are not obligated to complete the work, but neither are you free to desist from it.
quotes.notes: Rabbi Tarfon on perseverance in the endless labor of Torah and good deeds, from the Ethics of the Fathers.

quotes.content: The world stands on three things: on the Torah, on the service of God, and on acts of loving-kindness.
quotes.notes: A foundational teaching of Shimon the Righteous, one of the last of the Great Assembly, on the pillars that sustain the world.

quotes.content: Be of the disciples of Aaron, loving peace and pursuing peace, loving your fellow creatures, and drawing them near to the Torah.
quotes.notes: Hillel holds up Aaron the High Priest as the model peacemaker in the Ethics of the Fathers.

quotes.content: Say little and do much, and receive every person with a cheerful countenance.
quotes.notes: Shammai, Hillel's great contemporary, on humility of speech and warmth toward others.

quotes.content: Who is wise? One who learns from every person.
quotes.notes: The first of Ben Zoma's four questions in the Ethics of the Fathers, redefining wisdom as openness to learning.

quotes.content: Who is rich? One who is happy with his portion.
quotes.notes: Ben Zoma redefines wealth as contentment rather than possession, in the Ethics of the Fathers.

quotes.content: Who is mighty? One who conquers his own impulse.
quotes.notes: Ben Zoma locates true strength in self-mastery over one's inclinations, in the Ethics of the Fathers.

quotes.content: Who is honored? One who honors others.
quotes.notes: The last of Ben Zoma's four questions, teaching that honor is earned by giving it, in the Ethics of the Fathers.

quotes.content: Judge every person favorably.
quotes.notes: Yehoshua ben Perachiah on giving others the benefit of the doubt, from the Ethics of the Fathers.

quotes.content: Do not separate yourself from the community, and do not judge your fellow until you have reached his place.
quotes.notes: Hillel on solidarity and empathy, from the Ethics of the Fathers.

quotes.content: Turn it over, and turn it over, for everything is in it.
quotes.notes: Ben Bag Bag's praise of endless study of the Torah, which he says contains everything, from the Ethics of the Fathers.

quotes.content: Reflect on three things and you will not come to sin: know from where you came, where you are going, and before Whom you are destined to give account.
quotes.notes: Akavya ben Mahalalel's meditation on origin, destiny, and accountability, from the Ethics of the Fathers.

quotes.content: Run to perform even a minor mitzvah, for one mitzvah leads to another.
quotes.notes: Ben Azzai on how good deeds beget good deeds, from the Ethics of the Fathers.

quotes.content: Much have I learned from my teachers, more from my colleagues, but from my students most of all.
quotes.notes: A Talmudic teaching on how teaching deepens the teacher's own understanding.

quotes.content: The Second Temple was destroyed because of baseless hatred.
quotes.notes: The Talmud's diagnosis that sinat chinam, hatred without cause, brought down the Second Temple; rendered from the standard text.

quotes.content: It hath been told thee, O man, what is good, and what the LORD doth require of thee: only to do justly, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with thy God.
quotes.notes: Micah's celebrated summary of what God asks of humanity, quoted verbatim from the 1917 Jewish Publication Society translation.

quotes.content: Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself: I am the LORD.
quotes.notes: The commandment Rabbi Akiva called a great principle of the Torah, quoted from the 1917 Jewish Publication Society translation.

quotes.content: And thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy might.
quotes.notes: The core verse of the Shema, recited daily in Jewish prayer, quoted from the 1917 Jewish Publication Society translation.

quotes.content: Justice, justice shalt thou follow, that thou mayest live.
quotes.notes: The doubled call for justice, quoted from the 1917 Jewish Publication Society translation.

quotes.content: I have set before thee life and death, the blessing and the curse; therefore choose life.
quotes.notes: Moses' charge to Israel to choose life, quoted from the 1917 Jewish Publication Society translation.

quotes.content: And God created man in His own image, in the image of God created He him.
quotes.notes: The verse grounding human dignity in the divine image, quoted from the 1917 Jewish Publication Society translation.

quotes.content: To every thing there is a season, and a time to every purpose under the heaven.
quotes.notes: The opening of the famous meditation on time in Ecclesiastes, quoted from the 1917 Jewish Publication Society translation.

quotes.content: They shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruninghooks; nation shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war any more.
quotes.notes: Isaiah's vision of universal peace, quoted from the 1917 Jewish Publication Society translation.

quotes.content: Her ways are ways of pleasantness, and all her paths are peace.
quotes.notes: A verse in praise of wisdom and Torah, recited when the Torah scroll is returned to the ark; quoted from the 1917 Jewish Publication Society translation.

quotes.content: Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!
quotes.notes: A short psalm celebrating harmony, quoted from the 1917 Jewish Publication Society translation.

quotes.content: The greatest level of charity is to strengthen the hand of the poor by giving a gift, a loan, or work, so that he need never depend on others again.
quotes.notes: Maimonides' highest of eight levels of tzedakah, condensed from his code of law the Mishneh Torah, Laws of Gifts to the Poor.

quotes.content: Judaism is a religion of time aiming at the sanctification of time.
quotes.notes: Heschel's central thesis in The Sabbath, that Judaism builds cathedrals in time rather than space.

quotes.content: The Sabbaths are our great cathedrals.
quotes.notes: Heschel's image of the Sabbath as sacred architecture built in time, from The Sabbath.

quotes.content: Just to be is a blessing. Just to live is holy.
quotes.notes: Heschel on radical amazement and the sanctity of existence, from God in Search of Man.

quotes.content: I felt my legs were praying.
quotes.source: Reflecting on the Selma to Montgomery march (1965)
quotes.notes: Heschel's much-quoted description of marching for civil rights alongside Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. in Selma, Alabama.
